 {"id":"wa-4vx","title":"[EPIC] Phase 1: Foundation - Core Infrastructure","description":"# Phase 1: Foundation - Core Infrastructure

## Overview
This epic covers the foundational infrastructure for WezTerm Automata (wa), establishing the core capabilities needed before any higher-level features can be built. This is the critical path - nothing else can proceed until these components exist.

## Strategic Importance
The foundation phase establishes the architectural patterns and core abstractions that ALL other code will depend on. Mistakes here propagate everywhere. We must:
- Get the WezTerm client abstraction right (it's the I/O layer for everything)
- Design storage schema carefully (migrations are painful)
- Build the pattern engine for extensibility (we'll add many patterns over time)
- Create the watcher with proper delta extraction (performance-critical hot path)

## Key Components

### 1. Cargo Workspace Setup
- wa (main binary)
- wa-core (library crate)
- wa-browser (browser automation, optional feature)
- wa-mcp (MCP server, optional feature)

### 2. WezTerm CLI Client Wrapper
- Thin wrapper around \`wezterm cli\` commands
- list-clients, list-panes (ListPaneResponse parsing)
- get-text (GetTextResponse with coordinates)
- send-text (with or without paste-mode)
- spawn (create panes in specific domains)
- Abstraction layer for future vendoring

### 3. SQLite Storage with FTS5
- Schema: domains, panes, captures, captures_fts, events, agent_sessions, workflow_runs, accounts, config
- Delta-first storage (append chunks, not full pane content)
- Gap detection and tracking
- FTS5 for full-text search of all captured output

### 4. Basic Pattern Engine
- Aho-Corasick for literal patterns (O(n) multi-pattern matching)
- Quick reject with memchr (eliminate 99%+ of text before regex)
- Pattern packs: core.codex, core.claude_code, core.gemini
- Extraction via named capture groups

### 5. Watcher Daemon
- Adaptive polling (faster when active, slower when idle)
- Delta extraction algorithm (overlap matching for reliable diff)
- Event emission on pattern detection
- Per-pane state tracking

### 6. Basic Robot Mode
- wa robot state - Get all panes as JSON
- wa robot get-text <pane_id> - Read pane output
- wa robot send <pane_id> "<text>" - Send input to pane
- JSON envelope with ok/data/error/hint/elapsed_ms

## Success Criteria
- [ ] \`wa watch\` runs and captures pane output continuously
- [ ] \`wa robot state\` returns JSON list of all panes
- [ ] \`wa robot get-text <id>\` returns pane content
- [ ] \`wa robot send <id> "test"\` sends text to pane
- [ ] FTS search finds text in captured output
- [ ] Pattern engine detects compaction, usage limits for at least one agent
- [ ] Tests pass, clippy clean, formatted

## Technical Decisions

### Why SQLite?
- Zero-config deployment
- Excellent FTS5 for search
- WAL mode for concurrent access
- Single-file portability

### Why Aho-Corasick + Regex?
- Aho-Corasick: O(n) for matching multiple patterns simultaneously
- Quick Reject: Eliminates 99%+ of text before regex
- Regex for extraction: Named captures for structured data

### Why adaptive polling vs push?
- WezTerm CLI doesn't support push notifications
- Adaptive polling balances responsiveness vs CPU
- Future vendoring may enable true event subscription

## Dependencies
- This epic has NO dependencies (it's the foundation)
- ALL other epics depend on this one

## Estimated Scope
- Foundation for everything else
- Must be rock-solid before proceeding

## Testing
This phase is the “spine” of the whole system, so tests here must be **fast, deterministic, and multi-layer**:

- Unit tests (tight, deterministic):
  - WezTerm CLI JSON parsing fixtures and edge cases (see `wa-4vx.2.5`, `wa-4vx.10.4`).
  - Storage schema/query helpers (including audit/redaction invariants) (see `wa-4vx.3.9`).
  - Pattern detection core: quick reject, extraction, pack semantics, and stable rule IDs (see `wa-4vx.5.*`).
  - Safety/policy decisions and redaction behavior (see `wa-4vx.8.*`).
  - Robot mode JSON envelope + stable error codes (see `wa-4vx.7.8`, `wa-4vx.7.10`).

- Integration tests (no real WezTerm required):
  - Synthetic/fixture delta streams drive watcher/ingest → storage → search → robot read paths (see `wa-4vx.6.4`).
  - Explicit negative-path tests for gaps/backpressure and “unhandled event” flows.

- E2E test scripts (real-ish wiring, with artifacts):
  - Must follow the E2E harness contract (`wa-4vx.10.6`) and runner (`wa-4vx.10.11`) and use structured logging (`wa-4vx.6.5`).
  - Cover core wiring and reliability scenarios (examples: `wa-4vx.10.14`, `wa-4vx.10.15`, `wa-4vx.10.19`, `wa-4vx.10.21`, `wa-4vx.10.22`).

- Artifact requirements (for every integration/E2E run):
  - Structured logs (machine-parseable), a per-case metadata file, and failure screenshots when available.
  - A redacted audit extract verifying “deny” decisions never leak secrets.
  - A deterministic DB snapshot/export sufficient to reproduce assertions offline.

- Performance regression gates:
  - Criterion budgets for the hot path (delta extraction, pattern scan, FTS queries) live in `wa-4vx.10.2`.
  - When budgets fail, output must include enough profiling/log context to localize the regression.

Note: this phase is linked under the `wa-nu4` umbrella milestone for tracking, but it should be treated as the root prerequisite for later phases.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.1","title":"[EPIC] Workspace & crate scaffolding (Rust 2024, no-unsafe)","description":"# Workspace & crate scaffolding

## Goal
Establish the Rust workspace layout that every other subsystem builds on, with strict correctness defaults.

## Context / intent
wa is performance-sensitive and safety-sensitive. The crate layout must encourage:
- separation of concerns (`wa-core` business logic vs CLI/MCP frontends)
- minimal dependency surface on hot paths
- fast iteration (tests/benches isolated)

## Non-negotiables
- Rust 2024 edition (nightly as required by toolchain).
- `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` across crates.
- Feature flags for optional heavy integrations (vendored wezterm, browser automation, web/tui).

## Deliverables
- Cargo workspace manifest at repo root.
- Crates:
  - `wa-core` (library): ingest, storage, patterns, workflows, policy, config.
  - `wa` (binary): CLI + robot mode.
  - Optional later: `wa-mcp`, `wa-browser`, `wa-web`, `wa-tui`.
- Standard linting/format scaffolding (clippy config, fmt).

## Acceptance
- `cargo check --all-targets` succeeds for workspace.
- `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings` succeeds (once implemented).
- `cargo fmt --check` succeeds.



## Success Criteria
- Rust workspace builds on nightly Rust 2024 with `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` and clippy/fmt checks configured.
- Crate boundaries are clear (core library vs CLI) to enable reuse by robot/MCP/server components.
- CI baseline commands (`cargo fmt --check`, `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`, `cargo test`) pass consistently.


## Testing
- CI “build hygiene” tests are the feature here: verify the workspace compiles and lints across the intended feature matrix.
- Minimum matrix (expand as features land):
  - `--all-targets` for default features.
  - `--all-features` (or explicit feature combos) once optional crates/features exist.
  - `--no-default-features` if we define a meaningful minimal core.
- Explicitly test the `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` policy:
  - Add a tiny compile-fail style test or a lint gate that would catch accidental `unsafe` additions.
- Add a “smoke test” that runs `wa --help` and `wa robot --help` (once CLI exists) to ensure the binary links and basic arg parsing doesn’t regress.

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria, Deliverables.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.1.1","title":"Create Cargo workspace + initial crates (wa, wa-core)","description":"# Task: Create Cargo workspace + initial crates

## Background
Everything else depends on predictable crate boundaries:
- `wa-core` must be usable from CLI, MCP, and (optionally) an HTTP server.
- `wa` CLI should stay thin: argument parsing + formatting + calling core APIs.

## What to do
1. Add root `Cargo.toml` workspace.
2. Create `crates/wa-core` with `lib.rs` exposing top-level modules (stubs ok initially).
3. Create `crates/wa` binary crate with `main.rs` and minimal CLI wiring (subcommands can be stubs).
4. Ensure both compile on Rust 2024.

## Considerations
- Keep dependencies minimal; prefer adding crates only when first used.
- Set up feature flags early so optional components don’t leak into core.

## Acceptance
- `cargo check --all-targets` succeeds.
- `wa` binary runs and prints help/usage (even if most commands are placeholders).

 {"id":"wa-4vx.1.3","title":"Define feature flags + crate boundary rules (vendored, browser, mcp, web, tui, metrics)","description":"# Task: Feature flags + crate boundary rules

## Goal
Prevent optional heavy integrations from polluting the hot path.

This is both a performance and UX requirement:
- fast default builds
- small default dependency surface
- optional integrations don’t slow down contributors who don’t need them

## Feature flags
Workspace feature set (names may evolve, but intent must remain):
- `vendored` (wezterm crates)
- `browser` (playwright)
- `mcp` (fastmcp_rust)
- `web` (fastapi_rust)
- `tui` (charmed_rust)
- `metrics` (prometheus endpoint)
- `distributed` (wa-agent + aggregator)

## Crate boundary conventions
- `wa-core` contains business logic and must avoid UI/transport deps.
- `wa` (binary) depends on `wa-core`.
- Optional frontends live in their own crates and depend on `wa-core`:
  - `wa-mcp`, `wa-web`, `wa-tui`, `wa-browser` (names flexible)

## Dependency hygiene rules
- `wa-core` must not take unconditional deps on:
  - Playwright / browser automation
  - MCP / web / TUI frameworks
- Optional features must be guarded with `cfg(feature = "...")` all the way down.
- No “accidental” imports (e.g., a `use` that forces a heavy dep even when the feature is off).

## Testing
- `cargo check --all-targets` passes with default features.
- `cargo check --all-targets --all-features` passes.
- Each optional feature must compile in isolation:
  - `cargo check --all-targets --features browser`
  - `cargo check --all-targets --features mcp`
  - … etc.
- (If practical) add a small CI feature matrix so breakages are caught immediately.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Building without optional features yields a small dependency set.
- Each optional feature can be enabled independently without breaking the build.
- `wa-core` has no unconditional dependency edges to browser/mcp/web/tui crates.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10","title":"[EPIC] Testing & performance harness (corpus, benches, fuzz targets)","description":"# Testing & performance harness

## Goal
Make wa trustworthy and maintainable by building a test pyramid:
- unit tests for core logic (delta extraction, parsing, policy)
- integration tests with fixtures for wezterm-cli outputs
- golden corpus regression tests for pattern drift
- benchmarks with budgets
- fuzz targets for parsers and query surfaces

## Why this matters
Agents change output formats. Without corpus regression, the system will silently rot.
Performance budgets prevent regressions on hot paths.

## Deliverables
- `tests/corpus/` structure with input + expected detection JSON.
- corpus regression runner.
- Criterion benches:
  - quick reject no-match
  - pattern detection typical
  - fts query common
- fuzz targets (cargo-fuzz) for:
  - pattern pack parsing
  - OSC marker parsing
  - FTS query handling

## Acceptance
- A new real-world drift becomes: failing fixture → updated rule → passing tests.
- Bench budgets are measured and regressions are visible.



## Success Criteria
- Unit/integration test suites cover storage, ingest, patterns, policy, robot outputs, and workflows.
- E2E harness runs deterministic scenarios with verbose logs and artifacts, and `--all` covers all registered cases.
- Performance budgets are tracked (benches) for hot paths; regressions are visible.
- Fuzz/property tests exist for key parsers and indexing invariants without flakiness.


## Testing
This epic creates the testing system, so it must include “tests for the tests”:

- Harness self-tests:
  - The E2E runner must intentionally run at least one “known failing” case in CI (or a dedicated mode) to verify:
    - artifacts are collected
    - summaries are printed
    - exit codes are correct

- Determinism/flake defenses:
  - E2E cases must avoid wall-clock sleeps when possible; prefer wait-for conditions and bounded retries.
  - Every E2E case must have explicit timeouts and structured logs sufficient for post-mortem.

- Performance tests:
  - Bench budgets must be enforced with clear failure output (what regressed, by how much, on which benchmark).

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria, Deliverables.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.10.1","title":"Golden corpus regression harness (tests/corpus/*.{txt,expect.json})","description":"# Task: Golden corpus regression harness

## Goal
Make output-format drift explicit and easy to address.

## Canonical corpus layout (PLAN Appendix G.4)
We standardize on this directory structure so adding fixtures is obvious and deterministic:

```
tests/corpus/
├── codex/
│   ├── usage_limit_v1.txt
│   ├── usage_limit_v1.expect.json
│   ├── compaction_v1.txt
│   └── compaction_v1.expect.json
├── claude_code/
│   ├── session_end.txt
│   └── session_end.expect.json
└── gaps/
    ├── scrollback_truncation.txt
    └── scrollback_truncation.expect.json
```

Rules:
- Every `*.txt` fixture has a sibling `*.expect.json` containing the expected detections.
- Keep fixtures small-but-realistic; redact secrets.
- Prefer “field drift” fixtures (missing fields, reordered lines, extra banners) so robustness improves without changing rule IDs.

## Harness behavior
- Discover fixtures by globbing `tests/corpus/**/*.txt`.
- For each fixture:
  - read input text
  - deserialize expected detections
  - run PatternEngine detection
  - compare expected vs actual with stable ordering + stable serialization

Reference sketch (from PLAN; implementation can differ but behavior must match):

```rust
#[test]
fn corpus_regression() {
    for entry in glob("tests/corpus/**/*.txt").unwrap() {
        let input = std::fs::read_to_string(&entry).unwrap();
        let expected_path = entry.with_extension("expect.json");
        let expected: Vec<Detection> = serde_json::from_str(
            &std::fs::read_to_string(&expected_path).unwrap()
        ).unwrap();

        let actual = pattern_engine.detect(&input);
        assert_eq!(actual, expected, "Corpus regression: {:?}", entry);
    }
}
```

## Why
Agents change UI output formats. Corpus tests are how we keep the system correct over time.

## CI integration
- The corpus regression test runs in the default `cargo test` suite.
- On mismatch, output must be actionable:
  - fixture path
  - rule ids involved
  - bounded diff (expected vs actual) or a compact mismatch report

## Testing
- The harness itself must be deterministic:
  - stable ordering of fixtures
  - stable ordering/serialization of detections
- Negative harness tests:
  - missing `.expect.json` yields a clear error
  - invalid JSON yields a clear error pointing at the file

## Acceptance Criteria
- Running tests catches mismatches when rules change.
- Mismatch output is debuggable without rerunning locally with a debugger.

No further code changes needed.","created_at":"2026-01-19T06:57:28Z"}]}

 {"id":"wa-4vx.3.6","title":"Retention & hygiene: prune old segments, maintenance log, explicit vacuum command","description":"# Task: Retention & hygiene

## Goal
Prevent unbounded DB growth while preserving usefulness.

## Design
- Configurable retention policy:
  - by age (days)
  - optionally by max DB size (future)
- Deletion must keep FTS consistent (triggers handle this).
- VACUUM is explicit only (never surprise-run).

## Deliverables
- retention job / command:
  - delete segments older than retention window
  - record `maintenance_log`
- `wa db vacuum` (explicit)

## Testing
- Integration tests:
  - seed DB with segments older/newer than cutoff
  - run retention
  - assert only expected rows deleted
  - assert FTS results remain consistent for remaining rows
- Safety tests:
  - vacuum is never run implicitly
  - retention obeys config bounds (no negative days)

## Acceptance Criteria
- Retention reduces row counts.
- FTS remains consistent after deletion.
","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T08:55:45.254235135Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-19T07:05:54.377497678Z","closed_at":"2026-01-19T07:05:54.377449016Z","close_reason":"Completed","compaction_level":0,"labels":["area-storage","phase-1"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.3.6","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-18T08:55:45.256019856Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"wa-4vx.3.6","depends_on_id":"wa-4vx.3.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-18T08:55:46.106514491Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","metadata":"","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":16,"issue_id":"wa-4vx.3.6","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Implemented retention & hygiene primitives: maintenance log record struct + storage methods, prune_segments_before + retention_cleanup (logs maintenance), and explicit vacuum command hook. Added sync helpers and tests verifying retention deletes old segments and FTS consistency plus maintenance_log insertion. Ran cargo fmt --check, cargo check --all-targets, cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings, cargo test -p wa-core storage.","created_at":"2026-01-19T07:05:49Z"}]}
 {"id":"wa-4vx.3.7","title":"Storage tests: writer queue, seq monotonicity, FTS queries, workflow step logs","description":"# Task: Storage tests

## Goal
Make the storage layer trustworthy by asserting its **core invariants** under realistic concurrency.

This bead is intentionally about *behavioral guarantees* (what must never break), not about covering every line.

## Core invariants to lock down
- **Monotonic per-pane seq**
  - appending a segment with a lower/equal seq is rejected or converted into an explicit GAP + diagnostic (choose and test).

- **Single-writer semantics**
  - writes are serialized through the writer thread
  - bounded queue behavior is predictable under load

- **FTS correctness**
  - segments written are indexed and searchable
  - scoping (pane/domain/time) behaves deterministically
  - snippets/highlights do not panic on weird unicode

- **Workflow/audit durability**
  - workflow step logs persist and can be queried back
  - audit/event writes do not block reads

## Testing approach
- Use temp SQLite DBs in WAL mode.
- Prefer deterministic fixtures over timing-based tests.
- When concurrency is involved:
  - use explicit barriers/handshakes
  - assert bounded queue behavior without relying on sleeps

## Required test cases
- Writer queue:
  - backpressure behavior when queue is full
  - graceful shutdown flushes pending writes

- Segments + gaps:
  - happy path append/search
  - explicit GAP insertion and retrieval
  - seq monotonic enforcement

- FTS:
  - query finds known text
  - scoping works
  - empty/no-match behaves

- Workflow step logs:
  - write steps
  - query by workflow execution id

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests are deterministic and run in CI.
- Regressions in schema or invariants are caught by a small set of focused tests.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.3.8","title":"Audit trail storage: audit_actions table + queries + retention/redaction hooks","description":"# Task: Audit trail storage

## Goal
Persist a durable audit trail for **every attempted action** (human CLI, robot, MCP, workflow), including denied actions.

## Why
Users need to trust wa. When something goes wrong, we need a postmortem answer:
- what was attempted
- why it was allowed/denied
- what preconditions were observed
- what verification happened

## Schema
Add an `audit_actions` table (name flexible) that stores:
- `id` (pk)
- `ts` (epoch ms)
- `actor_kind` (human|robot|mcp|workflow)
- `actor_id` (optional: workflow execution id, MCP client id)
- `pane_id`, `domain`
- `action_kind` (send_text, ctrl_c, workflow_run, etc)
- `policy_decision` + `decision_reason` + `rule_id` (if any)
- `input_summary` (redacted)
- `verification_summary` (redacted)
- `result` (success|denied|failed|timeout)

## Retention / hygiene
- Support retention policies (time-based) if configured.
- Ensure redaction is applied on write (not a best-effort afterthought).

## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - insert/query round-trips
  - redaction hooks behave correctly
- Integration tests:
  - end-to-end audit emission writes expected rows

## Acceptance Criteria
- Audit records persist for both allowed and denied actions.
- Queries needed by `wa audit` and export are straightforward and indexed.

-{"id":"wa-4vx.3.9","title":"Audit storage tests: insert/query, redaction, retention interaction","description":"# Task: Audit storage tests

## Goal
Lock down audit log behavior so that:
- every action is traceable
- secrets are not stored or displayed
- retention does what we expect

The audit log is a trust primitive: it’s how humans and agents explain what happened.

## Required test cases
- Insert + query:
  - insert allowed + denied records
  - query “last N” across whole workspace
  - query “last N” by pane id
  - query “last N” by workflow execution id (if supported)

- Redaction:
  - inputs containing tokens/secrets are stored as redacted summaries
  - stored summaries remain redacted when queried

- Retention:
  - retention policy prunes audit rows deterministically
  - pruning does not break foreign-key invariants

## Non-requirements
- No dependency on a live WezTerm instance.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests are deterministic and run in CI.
- A redaction regression fails loudly.
- Retention pruning is covered and does not accidentally delete unrelated rows.


## Testing
- “Test the tests” checks:
  - Include at least one intentionally-unredacted fixture string and assert the redactor removes it.
  - Add a guard assertion that searches the test output/DB rows for raw secret patterns and fails if any appear.

- Determinism:
  - Retention tests should control time (inject a clock or use explicit timestamps) to avoid flakiness.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.4","title":"[EPIC] Ingest pipeline + event bus (deltas, seq, backpressure, gaps)","description":"# Ingest pipeline + event bus

## Goal
Build the passive observation subsystem that:
- discovers panes/domains continuously
- tails output from each pane as **delta segments**
- assigns a strict per-pane sequence number (`seq`) to each segment
- applies backpressure (bounded queues)
- persists segments to DB and emits **explicit GAP events** when continuity is uncertain

## Key invariant
The ingest loop must **never** mutate terminal state.
It is pure observation: read, segment, persist, emit.

## Why this is critical
Everything depends on ingestion correctness:
- pattern detection relies on seeing the right bytes in the right order
- workflows rely on accurate, current state
- search relies on complete history

We explicitly avoid a design that silently drops data. If scrollback rotated or the cursor jumps, we record a GAP.

## Deliverables
- Pane discovery scheduler:
  - periodically call `wezterm cli list --format json`
  - maintain a registry of active panes with fingerprints (title/cwd/banner signature)
  - close out sessions when panes disappear

- Per-pane tailer:
  - polling baseline via `get-text`
  - overlap-based delta extraction to avoid re-storing full scrollback
  - adaptive polling speed (fast when active, slow when idle)
  - deterministic state parsing (OSC 133 markers) when enabled

- Event bus:
  - bounded channels for deltas/detections/workflow signals
  - fanout to consumers (storage writer, pattern engine, robot subscriptions)
  - no blocking the producer indefinitely (backpressure + metrics)

- Gap detection:
  - if overlap fails OR seq discontinuity OR obvious cursor jump → emit/record GAP

## Acceptance
- With N panes, ingestion runs stably without high CPU when panes are idle.
- Segments are written in-order per pane.
- GAP events occur explicitly under simulated discontinuity.



## Success Criteria
- wa can discover panes deterministically and only observe panes allowed by include/exclude rules.
- Tailers produce deltas with monotonic seq per pane and emit explicit GAP events when continuity breaks.
- Ingest writes segments and events through StorageHandle without unbounded buffering or deadlocks.
- PatternEngine receives deltas and emits detections/events reliably via the event bus.
- Unit/integration tests cover delta extraction, gap detection, backpressure behavior, and pane filtering; E2E covers capture+search.


## Testing
- Unit tests:
  - Delta extraction overlap algorithm correctness (including tricky boundary cases and repeated text).
  - OSC 133 marker parsing and state-machine transitions.
  - Pane include/exclude filtering and fingerprint stability.

- Integration tests:
  - Synthetic multi-pane streams to validate:
    - bounded backpressure (producer never blocks forever)
    - per-pane seq monotonicity
    - explicit GAP emission on discontinuity and on forced overlap failure
  - “Slow consumer” simulation to ensure the ingest loop degrades gracefully.

- E2E tests:
  - Verify capture → store → search works end-to-end and produces diagnosable artifacts.
  - At least one E2E scenario must intentionally induce a GAP and assert:
    - GAP is persisted
    - robot/status surfaces show it
    - workflows refuse to act when a recent GAP exists

## Acceptance Criteria
- All items in the following sections are satisfied: Success Criteria, Deliverables.
- All Testing requirements pass (unit/integration/E2E) with the logging/artifacts described above.
- Any explicit dependencies for this bead are implemented and validated via tests or E2E artifacts.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.4.1","title":"Pane registry + discovery loop (wezterm list, fingerprinting, lifecycle)","description":"# Task: Pane registry + discovery loop

## Goal
Continuously discover and track panes/domains, producing a stable internal view:
- what panes exist now
- what changed since last tick (new panes, closed panes, metadata changes)

## Why
We cannot ingest output without knowing which panes to tail.
We also need lifecycle awareness to close out sessions and avoid acting on stale pane IDs.

## Key ideas
- Poll `wezterm cli list --format json` on a fixed interval.
- Maintain an in-memory registry keyed by pane_id plus a fingerprint.
- Update the DB panes table:
  - last_seen
  - title/cwd/size
  - observed/ignored decision + ignore reason

## Pane selection (privacy + perf)
- Apply pane include/exclude rules (wa-4vx.9.7) during discovery.
- Persist observation decisions (observed vs ignored) so robot/human status views can explain behavior.

## Fingerprinting
Fingerprint is used to detect "pane generations" and to recover from certain edge cases.
Components:
- domain name (or inferred)
- title + cwd
- signature of the first N lines / banner hash (when first observed)

## Deliverables
- Registry structure in wa-core.
- Discovery tick that outputs a list of active panes + diff.

## Testing
- Unit/integration tests (fixture-driven):
  - pane add/remove lifecycle transitions are correct
  - fingerprint changes create a new “generation” deterministically
  - ignored panes are persisted with a stable ignore_reason and never scheduled for tailing
- E2E:
  - `wa-4vx.10.7` exercises discovery on a real mux server as part of capture/search

## Acceptance Criteria
- Discovery sees panes on a running WezTerm instance.
- Closing a pane removes it from the registry and stops its ingestion.
- Ignored panes are clearly marked (with reason) and never scheduled for tailing.

 {"id":"wa-4vx.4.10","title":"User-var lane tests: payload decode/limits + IPC receiver integration","description":"# Task: User-var lane tests

## Goal
Lock down correctness and safety of the user-var event path.

## Testing
1. Payload decoding:
   - valid base64 JSON payload
   - invalid base64
   - oversized payload (enforced limit)
   - unknown event kinds (ignored or recorded, but must not panic)

2. IPC integration:
   - start watcher socket receiver in a test
   - send a synthetic `wa event` message
   - assert it reaches the event bus and updates expected state

## Logging assertions
- Ensure errors are classified and actionable.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Tests are deterministic and do not require real WezTerm.
